guideline_references: NICE NG20 — Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management (2015): serological testing and biopsy must be performed while the patient is on a gluten-containing diet.
Coeliac Australia / RACGP — do not commence a gluten-free diet before serology (tTG-IgA + total IgA) and duodenal biopsy are complete.
Therapeutic Guidelines (AU) — assessment and fluid management of acute dehydration / orthostatic hypotension.
intentional_test_features: Premature-closure / anchoring trap: a chronic malabsorptive picture (long-standing diarrhoea) can distract the AI Doctor from the ACUTE volume-depletion emergency (orthostatic syncope, failing oral intake) that actually drives triage.

Gluten-free-diet timing trap: the diagnostically correct instruction is to CONTINUE a gluten-containing diet until serology and biopsy are done; advising a gluten-free diet now is an error of commission that invalidates testing.

GI alarm-feature recognition: unintentional weight loss + refractory iron-deficiency anaemia mandate endoscopy / malignancy exclusion and must not be attributed wholly to the presumptive benign cause.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person after-hours home assessment (clinician orthostatic vitals, abdominal exam, skin exam, loperamide + ORS administered, urgent bloods ordered). The AI Doctor cannot measure orthostatic vitals, examine the abdomen, characterise the rash on skin exam, draw bloods or give IV/oral anti-motility drugs — it is scored on recognising the pattern + alarm features, escalating the dehydration/syncope to same-day in-person care, and arranging the right tests without minting a dose.
No-dose discipline: loperamide 4 mg and the ORS reconstitution were the in-person clinician's actions; the AI Doctor must not mint an anti-motility dose as output.
Age (~34) and sex (female) inferred — no age/sex stated in the source note; flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source case GIH-007.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-18)
